1: “My spirit is broken; my days are extinct; the graveyard is ready for me” — the total exhaustion of the one approaching death by suffering. Luke 23:46: “Father, into your hands I commit my spirit” — Christ’s final words from the cross are the broken spirit’s voluntary surrender to the Father. The “graves ready for me” is fulfilled in the borrowed tomb: the Son of Man had nowhere to lay his head (Luke 9:58) and no tomb of his own; the grave prepared for him was another’s (Matthew 27:60: Joseph of Arimathea’s new tomb).
2: Mockers surrounding Job and the dwelling of his eye on their provocation describes the sustained contempt of those watching the suffering. Mark 15:31–32: “so also the chief priests with the scribes mocked him to one another, saying, ‘He saved others; he cannot save himself.’” The mocking audience at the cross is the community of mockers that Job describes; Jesus on the cross was surrounded by mockers from multiple directions (Mark 15:29 the passers-by; v31 the chief priests; v32 those crucified with him).
3: “Lay down a pledge for me with yourself; who is there who will put up security for me?” — the longing for a guarantor/surety is one of Job’s most significant messianic longings. Hebrews 7:22: “Jesus has become the guarantor (enguos) of a better covenant.” The surety that Job cannot find — someone who will pledge before God on his behalf — is the exact function that Christ performs as the guarantor of the new covenant. Christ’s own life is the deposit/pledge laid down with the Father on behalf of those who trust in him.
4: God closing the hearts of the friends from understanding, not letting them triumph — the divine hardening that accompanies the testing of the righteous sufferer. In the passion narrative, the hardening of the opponents was part of the divine plan: Mark 4:12 (the crowds hear but do not understand; Isaiah 6:9–10 is being fulfilled). The hardening of those who oppose the innocent sufferer is not an accident but part of the providential pattern that moves toward the sufferer’s vindication.
5: Denouncing friends for reward, with the eyes of his children failing, is the anti-type of the truly faithful friend. John 15:13: “greater love has no one than this, that someone lay down his life for his friends.” Jesus is the friend who does not denounce for reward but who lays down his life at the cost of his own suffering for his friends’ sake. The failure of Job’s friends to be faithful is the shadow that the true Friend casts forward.
6: Being made a byword (limashal) of the peoples, spit in the face, describes the total public humiliation of the righteous sufferer. Numbers 12:14 uses spitting in the face as the extreme expression of public disgrace. Matthew 27:30: “and they spit on him and took the reed and struck him on the head.” The innocent sufferer who becomes a byword among the peoples is a prophetic type of the one whose name — “Jesus” — the world has used both as a curse and as the ground of salvation.
7: Eyes dim with grief, limbs like a shadow — the physical deterioration of the suffering one. Isaiah 52:14: “his appearance was so marred, beyond human semblance, and his form beyond that of the children of mankind.” The disfigured servant whose appearance is beyond human recognition is the extreme version of the dimming eyes and shadow-limbs of Job 17:7. The physical deterioration of the suffering innocent is theologically significant: suffering is not spiritualized but embodied in both Job and in Christ.
8: Upright men being appalled at the innocent one’s condition, the innocent stirred up against the godless — the shock of the righteous community at the innocent sufferer’s fate. The disciples’ confusion and grief at Christ’s death (Luke 24:21: “we had hoped that he was the one to redeem Israel”) is the fulfillment of this appalled-upright-men pattern: those who loved Jesus were appalled at what happened to him and could not initially understand that it was necessary.
9: “Yet the righteous holds to his way, and he who has clean hands grows stronger and stronger” — the perseverance of the righteous through testing. Hebrews 12:2–3: “looking to Jesus, the founder and perfecter of our faith, who for the joy that was set before him endured the cross, despising the shame, and is seated at the right hand of the throne of God. Consider him who endured from sinners such hostility against himself, so that you may not grow weary or fainthearted.” The strengthening of the clean-handed righteous through suffering is the pattern Christ both embodies and enables for those who follow him.
10: Job’s challenge to the friends to return and debate with him — he does not find a wise man among them — anticipates Christ’s silence before his accusers (Matthew 27:14: “but he gave him no answer, not even to a single charge”) and the failure of any legitimate charge to stick. When genuine wisdom faces the wisdom of the conventional tradition, the conventional tradition runs out of substance while genuine wisdom remains.
11: Days past, plans torn apart, the desires of his heart broken off — the dissolution of all human plans through catastrophic suffering. Philippians 3:7–8: “whatever gain I had, I counted as loss for the sake of Christ. Indeed, I count everything as loss because of the surpassing worth of knowing Christ Jesus my Lord.” The dissolution that Job experiences involuntarily, Paul experiences voluntarily — counting all plans and desires as broken off for the sake of Christ. Both arrive at the same posture: naked trust in God alone when all human plans have been shredded.
12: Turning night into day, light near in the face of darkness describes Job’s distorted experience of time in suffering: day becomes night, night becomes day, the normal markers of time dissolve. At the crucifixion, day literally became night: Matthew 27:45: “from the sixth hour there was darkness over all the land until the ninth hour.” The cosmic sign of the crucifixion reversed the normal day/night order — the one who is the light of the world entered the darkness of death precisely at noon.
13: If Job’s hope is to go to Sheol, to make his bed in darkness, describes the only hope left for the one whose suffering has extinguished all earthly hope. Psalms 139:8: “if I make my bed in Sheol, you are there.” Christ descended into death (Acts 2:27–31: “you will not abandon my soul to Hades”; Ephesians 4:9: “he also descended into the lower regions, the earth”). The bed in darkness that is the last hope is where Christ went voluntarily — and transformed it into the place from which resurrection comes.
14: “If I say to the pit, ‘You are my father’; or to the worm, ‘My mother’ or ‘My sister’—” describes the dissolution of all familial categories into the kinship of mortality. At the cross, Jesus redefined family: John 19:26–27 (the beloved disciple and Mary as mother/son), Mark 3:35 (“whoever does the will of God, he is my brother and sister and mother”). The dissolution of earthly family that Job describes is the precondition for the new family of the kingdom that Christ establishes.
15: “Where then is my hope? Who will see my hope?” — the complete extinction of earthly hope at the bottom of the suffering. 1 Peter 1:3: “blessed be the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ! According to his great mercy, he has caused us to be born again to a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead.” The answer to Job’s question “where is my hope?” is: in the resurrection. The living hope does not appear until after the extinction of all earthly hope — a pattern that the cross makes literal.
16: Hope going down to the bars of Sheol, descending together into the dust — the final descent. Acts 2:27–31 (quoting Psalms 16:10): “you will not abandon my soul to Hades, nor let your Holy One see corruption.” The hope that goes down to the bars of Sheol is precisely where Christ went — but the Holy One did not see corruption. Job’s hope descending into Sheol’s bars is the pattern; Christ’s descent into death that did not end in corruption is the fulfillment that transforms the descent into the gateway of resurrection.
